preFix.title: Building a rain garden for driveway and hard-standing runoff
preFix.subtitle: A planted depression that captures, filters, and infiltrates surface water
preFix.excerpt: A rain garden is a planted shallow depression positioned to receive runoff from hard surfaces, driveways, paths, roof downpipes. Water flows in during rain, pools briefly, infiltrates through a permeable soil layer, and is taken up by plants adapted to temporary flooding. It prevents that water entering the combined sewer or flowing down the street. A typical front-garden rain garden takes a weekend to build.
preFix.sourceNotes: CIRIA SuDS Manual 2015 (C753) for rain garden design principles. gov.uk Planning Policy Statement for SuDS. RHS rain garden plant selection guidance 2024. Susdrain case studies for residential rain gardens.

content.text: Rain falling on a paved driveway cannot soak in. It runs into the gutter and then into the storm or combined sewer. In a heavy downpour, this contributes to flash flooding and sewer overflow. A

at the driveway edge or at a downpipe outlet intercepts the runoff and lets it soak into the ground. This delivers water to plant roots and the water table rather than the sewer. A well-designed rain garden on a 50 m2 driveway captures around 1,200 litres during a medium-intensity UK rainstorm.
Before building a rain garden, check the

of the proposed site. Dig a 300 mm deep test hole, fill it with water, and time how long it takes to drain empty. If it drains within 24 hours, the infiltration rate is sufficient. If it holds water for 48 hours or more, the subsoil is heavy clay and a rain garden will not work without drainage enhancement (a perforated drainpipe to a soakaway, or a gravel layer above the clay).

A rain garden should be approximately 10 to 20% of the area it drains. For a 50 m2 driveway, a 5 to 10 m2 rain garden. The standard depth is 150 to 300 mm of ponding zone (the depth of water that sits in the depression during a rain event before infiltrating). Shallower means more frequent inundation but shorter plants; deeper holds more water and can manage larger storm events but stresses plants if drainage is slow.
Mark the rain garden area with a rope or spray paint. It should be positioned downhill from the driveway and at least 1.5 m from any building foundation.
Excavate to 400 mm depth. Remove all topsoil and set it aside for reuse in the growing medium mix. Remove subsoil to the bottom of the excavation.

Backfill with rain garden growing medium: 50% topsoil (the material you removed), 30% sharp sand, 20% garden compost. Mix thoroughly. The sand improves infiltration; the compost supports plant establishment. Firm lightly, do not compact.
Shape the entry channel. Cut a shallow V-notch at the uphill edge where runoff enters, typically 150 mm wide and 75 mm deep. Stone-line this entry channel to prevent erosion.

Create an overflow point. At the downhill edge, leave a 100 mm gap in the berm or place a large flat stone as a weir. If the rain garden fills completely in an extreme event, the overflow routes water away from the building, not toward it.

Rain garden plants must tolerate both temporary inundation (48 hours of standing water) and normal dry periods between rain events. UK plants that suit the inundation zone include: purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria), iris (Iris pseudacorus, I. sibirica), sedges (Carex pendula, C. riparia), and ragged robin (Lychnis flos-cuculi). On the margins above the ponding zone, which stay moist but not flooded: astrantia, meadow cranesbill (Geranium pratense), and most native wildflowers.
